source_paper_title: Lower Complexity Bounds of First-Order Methods for Affinely Constrained Composite Nonconvex Problems
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Liu_2025, title={Lower Complexity Bounds of First-Order Methods for Affinely Constrained Composite Nonconvex Problems}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0377}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0377}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Liu, Wei and Lin, Qihang and Xu, Yangyang}, year={2025}, month=june }
context:
Section 4 (Conclusion and Open Questions), page 25.

First, for Algorithm Class 1 on solving Problem Class 1, can the lower bound \(O(\kappa([\bar A;A])L_f\Delta F_0\varepsilon^{-2})\) be achieved, i.e., is it tight?
title: Achieve tight oracle complexity for composite nonconvex optimization with affine constraints
background_latex:
Consider the affinely constrained composite optimization problem
\[
\min_{x\in\mathbb{R}^d} F_0(x):= f_0(x)+g(x)\quad\text{s.t.}\quad Ax+b=0.
\]
Here \(A\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times d}\), \(b\in\mathbb{R}^n\). The smooth term \(f_0:\mathbb{R}^d\to\mathbb{R}\) has \(L_f\)-Lipschitz gradient:
\[
\|\nabla f_0(x)-\nabla f_0(x')\|\le L_f\|x-x'\|,\quad \forall x,x'.
\]
The regularizer \(g:\mathbb{R}^d\to\mathbb{R}\cup\{+\infty\}\) is proper, lower semicontinuous, convex, and may be nonsmooth. In the problem class of interest, \(g\) has the compositional form
\[
 g(x)=\bar g(\bar A x+\bar b),
\]
with \(\bar A\in\mathbb{R}^{\bar n\times d}\), \(\bar b\in\mathbb{R}^{\bar n}\), and \(\bar g:\mathbb{R}^{\bar n}\to\mathbb{R}\cup\{+\infty\}\) proper l.s.c. convex, with \(\mathrm{relint}(\mathrm{dom}(\bar g))\neq\emptyset\) and \(\mathrm{dom}(\bar g)\) not a singleton.

An \(\varepsilon\)-stationary point (for \(\varepsilon\ge 0\)) is defined via existence of a multiplier \(\gamma\in\mathbb{R}^n\) such that
\[
\max\Big\{\mathrm{dist}\big(0,\nabla f_0(x)+A^\top\gamma+\partial g(x)\big),\ \|Ax+b\|\Big\}\le \varepsilon,
\]
where \(\partial g(x)\) is the convex subdifferential and \(\mathrm{dist}(0,S):=\inf_{s\in S}\|s\|\).

A first-order method (FOM) measures cost by oracle calls. In Algorithm Class 1, at query \((x,z,\eta)\) an oracle returns
\(\nabla f_0(x), Ax, A^\top z, \mathrm{prox}_{\eta g}(x)\), where
\[
\mathrm{prox}_{\eta g}(x):=\arg\min_{x'}\Big\{g(x')+\tfrac{1}{2\eta}\|x'-x\|^2\Big\}.
\]
The paper proves a lower bound for Algorithm Class 1: there exist instances requiring \(\Omega\big(\kappa([\bar A;A])\,L_f\,\Delta F_0\,\varepsilon^{-2}\big)\) oracle calls, where \([\bar A;A]\) denotes row-stacking and
\[
\kappa([\bar A;A]) := \sqrt{\frac{\lambda_{\max}([\bar A;A][\bar A;A]^\top)}{\lambda_{\min}^+([\bar A;A][\bar A;A]^\top)}}
\]
(with \(\lambda_{\min}^+\) the smallest positive eigenvalue), and \(\Delta F_0 := F_0(x^{(0)})-\inf_x F_0(x)\).

An upper bound matching this (up to logarithmic factors) is known for a different oracle/model (Algorithm Class 2 / a splitting reformulation), but for Algorithm Class 1 the paper does not provide a matching algorithmic upper bound and explicitly leaves tightness unresolved.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether there exists a first-order method in Algorithm Class 1 (i.e., using oracle access to \(\nabla f_0(x)\), \(Ax\), \(A^\top z\), and \(\mathrm{prox}_{\eta g}(x)\)) that, for every instance in the stated class, outputs a point \(x\in\mathbb{R}^d\) satisfying
\[
\max\Big\{\mathrm{dist}\big(0,\nabla f_0(x)+A^\top\gamma+\partial g(x)\big),\ \|Ax+b\|\Big\}\le \varepsilon
\]
for some \(\gamma\in\mathbb{R}^n\), using at most \(\tilde O\big(\kappa([\bar A;A])\,L_f\,\Delta F_0\,\varepsilon^{-2}\big)\) oracle calls (i.e., matching the proved lower bound \(\Omega\big(\kappa([\bar A;A])\,L_f\,\Delta F_0\,\varepsilon^{-2}\big)\) up to logarithmic factors).
latex:
Consider the affinely constrained composite optimization problem
\[
\min_{x\in\mathbb{R}^d} F_0(x):= f_0(x)+g(x)\quad\text{s.t.}\quad Ax+b=0.
\]
Here \(A\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times d}\), \(b\in\mathbb{R}^n\). The smooth term \(f_0:\mathbb{R}^d\to\mathbb{R}\) has \(L_f\)-Lipschitz gradient:
\[
\|\nabla f_0(x)-\nabla f_0(x')\|\le L_f\|x-x'\|,\quad \forall x,x'.
\]
The regularizer \(g:\mathbb{R}^d\to\mathbb{R}\cup\{+\infty\}\) is proper, lower semicontinuous, convex, and may be nonsmooth. In the problem class of interest, \(g\) has the compositional form
\[

\]
with \(\bar A\in\mathbb{R}^{\bar n\times d}\), \(\bar b\in\mathbb{R}^{\bar n}\), and \(\bar g:\mathbb{R}^{\bar n}\to\mathbb{R}\cup\{+\infty\}\) proper l.s.c. convex, with \(\mathrm{relint}(\mathrm{dom}(\bar g))\neq\emptyset\) and \(\mathrm{dom}(\bar g)\) not a singleton.

An \(\varepsilon\)-stationary point (for \(\varepsilon\ge 0\)) is defined via existence of a multiplier \(\gamma\in\mathbb{R}^n\) such that
\[

where \(\partial g(x)\) is the convex subdifferential and \(\mathrm{dist}(0,S):=\inf_{s\in S}\|s\|\).

\(\nabla f_0(x), Ax, A^\top z, \mathrm{prox}_{\eta g}(x)\), where
\[
\mathrm{prox}_{\eta g}(x):=\arg\min_{x'}\Big\{g(x')+\tfrac{1}{2\eta}\|x'-x\|^2\Big\}.
\]
The paper proves a lower bound for Algorithm Class 1: there exist instances requiring \(\Omega\big(\kappa([\bar A;A])\,L_f\,\Delta F_0\,\varepsilon^{-2}\big)\) oracle calls, where \([\bar A;A]\) denotes row-stacking and
\[
\kappa([\bar A;A]) := \sqrt{\frac{\lambda_{\max}([\bar A;A][\bar A;A]^\top)}{\lambda_{\min}^+([\bar A;A][\bar A;A]^\top)}}
\]
(with \(\lambda_{\min}^+\) the smallest positive eigenvalue), and \(\Delta F_0 := F_0(x^{(0)})-\inf_x F_0(x)\).

An upper bound matching this (up to logarithmic factors) is known for a different oracle/model (Algorithm Class 2 / a splitting reformulation), but for Algorithm Class 1 the paper does not provide a matching algorithmic upper bound and explicitly leaves tightness unresolved.

Determine whether there exists a first-order method in Algorithm Class 1 (i.e., using oracle access to \(\nabla f_0(x)\), \(Ax\), \(A^\top z\), and \(\mathrm{prox}_{\eta g}(x)\)) that, for every instance in the stated class, outputs a point \(x\in\mathbb{R}^d\) satisfying
\[
\max\Big\{\mathrm{dist}\big(0,\nabla f_0(x)+A^\top\gamma+\partial g(x)\big),\ \|Ax+b\|\Big\}\le \varepsilon
\]
for some \(\gamma\in\mathbb{R}^n\), using at most \(\tilde O\big(\kappa([\bar A;A])\,L_f\,\Delta F_0\,\varepsilon^{-2}\big)\) oracle calls (i.e., matching the proved lower bound \(\Omega\big(\kappa([\bar A;A])\,L_f\,\Delta F_0\,\varepsilon^{-2}\big)\) up to logarithmic factors).
open_problem_quote: First, for Algorithm Class 1 on solving Problem Class 1, can the lower bound \(O(\kappa([\bar A;A])L_f\Delta F_0\varepsilon^{-2})\) be achieved, i.e., is it tight?
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement is existential/universal over all instances and asks for an algorithm matching a lower bound, so there is no finite instance space where a counterexample would decisively refute it. One could test candidate methods empirically on constructed hard instances, but failure would not constitute a proof of impossibility.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can parameterize candidate Algorithm Class 1 methods (e.g., primal-dual/prox-penalty schemes with tunable stepsizes) and use computable stationarity residuals as a fitness signal on families of hard instances. However, the objective is worst-case complexity (asymptotic in \(\varepsilon\)), so empirical search can suggest algorithms or regimes but cannot certify tightness without additional theoretical analysis.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The Liu--Lin--Xu paper (arXiv:2502.17770v2; extended arXiv:2307.07605) establishes a lower oracle-complexity bound for Algorithm Class 1 (ORACLE1 access to \(\nabla f_0(x)\), \(Ax\), \(A^\top z\), and \(\mathrm{prox}_{\eta g}(x)\)) on Problem Class 1: there exist instances requiring \(\Omega(\kappa([\bar A;A])\,L_f\,\Delta F_0\,\varepsilon^{-2})\) oracle calls to reach an \(\varepsilon\)-stationary point. The hard instance is built by splitting a tridiagonal Toeplitz constraint operator \(H\) into two parts: one part remains as explicit affine constraints \(Ax=0\), while the other is encoded into a nonsmooth term \(g(x)=\beta\sum_{i\in\mathcal M}\|x_i-x_{i+1}\|_1\). This construction forces a slow expansion of support in the iterates (a “zero-respecting” phenomenon), yielding the \(\kappa([\bar A;A])\) dependence and showing that nonsmooth regularization can make the affinely constrained problem strictly harder than the smooth case even when \(\mathrm{prox}_{\eta g}\) is available.

Among the limited forward-citation evidence provided here, two augmented-Lagrangian-type works offer only partial progress toward the open tightness question for Algorithm Class 1. The linearized augmented Lagrangian method paper proves \(O(\varepsilon^{-2})\) complexity for smooth nonconvex equality-constrained problems and, in the affine case, obtains a condition-number dependence \(\kappa_A^2\) (with \(\kappa_A=\|A\|/\sigma_{\min}(A)\)), explicitly suggesting that acceleration/extrapolation might reduce this to \(\kappa_A\). The proximal augmented Lagrangian method paper establishes finite \(\varepsilon\)-KKT termination for problems with proximable terms and nonlinear constraints but does not provide worst-case oracle complexity estimates comparable to \(\tilde O(\kappa([\bar A;A])L_f\Delta F_0\varepsilon^{-2})\).

As of the provided citation set, no paper resolves whether an Algorithm Class 1 method can match the Liu--Lin--Xu lower bound up to logarithmic factors for all instances in Problem Class 1. The best-known near-optimality result in the Liu--Lin--Xu line is for Algorithm Class 2 (splitting reformulation with ORACLE2), where an inexact proximal-gradient-type method achieves \(\tilde O(\kappa([\bar A;A])L_f\Delta F_0\varepsilon^{-2})\) under additional assumptions (e.g., full row rank of \([\bar A;A]\) and Lipschitz \(\bar g\)). Bridging the gap for Algorithm Class 1 likely requires new acceleration mechanisms that exploit \(\mathrm{prox}_{\eta g}\) while controlling feasibility \(\|Ax+b\|\) without incurring extra \(\kappa\) factors, or alternatively a refined lower bound showing that ORACLE1 intrinsically cannot attain the \(\kappa([\bar A;A])\) scaling in the worst case.

sources.title: Complexity of a linearized augmented Lagrangian method for nonconvex minimization with nonlinear equality constraints
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proposes a Linearized Augmented Lagrangian (L-AL) method for smooth nonconvex problems with nonlinear equality constraints \(\min_x f(x)\ \text{s.t. }F(x)=0\). Under local smoothness and LICQ-type assumptions plus a dynamic regularization rule, it proves global convergence to KKT points and an iteration complexity of \(K=O(\sqrt{\rho}\,\varepsilon^{-2})\) Jacobian evaluations to obtain an \(\varepsilon\)-first-order solution (Theorem 3). In the affine-constraint special case \(F(x)=Ax-b\) with full row rank \(A\), it derives \(K=O(\kappa_A^2 L_f (\bar P-\underline P)\,\varepsilon^{-2})\) matrix–vector multiplications with \(A,A^T\), where \(\kappa_A=\|A\|/\sigma_{\min}(A)\) (Corollary 1), and notes that achieving \(\kappa_A\) dependence may require acceleration/extrapolation (Remark 6).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for an Algorithm Class 1 method (oracle: \(\nabla f_0(x),Ax,A^Tz,\mathrm{prox}_{\eta g}(x)\)) for affinely constrained composite nonconvex problems \(f_0+g\) with \(g(x)=\bar g(\bar A x+\bar b)\), matching the lower bound \(\tilde O(\kappa([\bar A;A])L_f\Delta F_0\varepsilon^{-2})\). This citing paper studies a different setting: smooth objective \(f\) and (generally nonlinear) equality constraints \(F(x)=0\), with no nonsmooth composite term \(g\) and no proximal-oracle model. Its complexity is measured in Jacobian evaluations and, in the affine case, yields \(\kappa_A^2\) dependence rather than the desired \(\kappa([\bar A;A])\) (and it explicitly conjectures that extrapolation might reduce \(\kappa_A^2\) to \(\kappa_A\)). Thus it does not resolve the tightness question for Algorithm Class 1, but provides related augmented-Lagrangian/linearization techniques and a complexity result in a neighboring (smooth) constrained model.

sources.title: A proximal augmented Lagrangian method for nonconvex optimization with equality and inequality constraints
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proposes an inexact proximal augmented Lagrangian method (P-BALM) for nonconvex problems with equality/inequality constraints and a proximable convex term. Under Assumptions I–II (including existence of an initial feasible point), Theorem 3.1 shows finite termination at an \(\varepsilon\)-KKT point: for any \(\varepsilon>\tau\), after finitely many outer iterations there exists \((x,\lambda,\mu)\) with \(\|x-\mathrm{prox}_{f_2}(x-\nabla_x L(x,\lambda,\mu))\|_\infty\le \varepsilon\), \(\|h(x)\|_\infty\le \varepsilon\), \(\|\max\{0,g(x)\}\|_\infty\le \varepsilon\), and approximate complementarity (via \(E_k\)).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a tight (up to logs) oracle complexity upper bound \(\tilde O(\kappa([\bar A;A])L_f\Delta F_0\varepsilon^{-2})\) for Algorithm Class 1 (oracle access to \(\nabla f_0\), \(Ax\), \(A^\top z\), \(\mathrm{prox}_{\eta g}\)) on affinely constrained composite nonconvex problems. This paper studies (proximal) augmented Lagrangian methods for a broader nonlinear-constraint model and proves finite \(\varepsilon\)-KKT termination, but it does not provide a worst-case oracle/iteration complexity bound in terms of \(L_f,\Delta F_0,\kappa([\bar A;A])\) and \(\varepsilon\), nor does it specialize to the Algorithm Class 1 oracle model or match the \(\varepsilon^{-2}\) lower bound. The authors explicitly list “development of iteration complexity estimates” as future work.
